input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle follows a structured MBSE process, progressing through distinct abstraction levels: requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical implementation. Each level is represented with suitable modeling languages—SysML for higher‑level architecture, Acme, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component‑connector descriptions—and behavior is refined using state‑machine or automaton models (MontiArc Automaton, SysML v1.7 state machines). Transitions between stages are managed by synchronizing models across levels and performing consistency checks with the physical system, ensuring traceability. Governance of the lifecycle is achieved through model validation, simulation (e.g., Simulink), and code generation in C++/Java to realize the product layer, thereby maintaining alignment between virtual representations and the physical counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for the Digital Twin employs MBSE methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT, integrating SysML, Acme, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs to define components, interfaces, and interconnections. Behavioral modeling is performed using state‑machines or automata, followed by detailed simulation in MathWorks Simulink. Implementation is achieved via code generation in C++ or Java. Quality assurance is addressed through simulation‑based validation and consistency checks between logical and product models. Evolution is managed by maintaining separate abstraction models and re‑using components across versions; however, explicit version‑control schemes, testing, or validation procedures are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The Digital Twin system implements data governance following the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) specifications detailed in Dokument 50 (Plattform Industrie 4.0 – Details of the Asset Administration Shell).  The AAS defines clear data ownership metadata and access‑control lists (ACLs) that enforce ownership transfer and responsibility at the asset level.  Data privacy is protected through mandatory encryption of all data streams using the MQTT protocol (see Dokument 52 – Mosquitto implementation) with TLS 1.2 or higher, ensuring confidentiality and integrity during transmission.  User authentication is performed via username/password or X.509 certificates (as described in Mosquitto), and role‑based access control in the AAS restricts data visibility to authorized stakeholders.  All personal or sensitive data is anonymised according to ISO 27018‑compatible practices before storage in the AAS registry, and consent management is handled by the IoT platform Thingsboard (Dokument 53), which supports GDPR‑aligned consent workflows and audit logging of data access events.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Security: The system leverages the MQTT protocol with TLS encryption, username/password and optional client certificates for secure communication (Dokument 52).  The interface with the AAS is protected by role‑based access control and signed JSON Web Tokens (JWT) issued by Thingsboard (Dokument 53).  Threat mitigation includes continuous monitoring of anomalous connection patterns as recommended in the RAMI 4.0 reference architecture (Dokument 49) and the benefits/pitfalls analysis (Dokument 48).  Safety: The application adopts the safety domain of RAMI 4.0, implementing fail‑safe mechanisms such as watchdog timers and redundancy in critical signal paths.  Explicit risk assessments are performed following RAMI 4.0 guidelines, and safety‑critical actuators interface via the Functional Mockup Interface (FMI) defined in Dokument 51 to enable model‑based safety verification.  The Digital Twin further adopts synchronized time stamping and integrity checks on simulation data to prevent spurious state updates, thereby ensuring both cyber‑physical security and operational safety.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
